notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
As a freelancer, you’re basically running your own benefits department. You can’t copy every employer perk, but you can get very close with the right accounts and a few simple systems.

Here’s a menu of what many freelancers set up and how it all fits together.

1. Health Insurance & Medical Costs
A. Health insurance options (US)

Common paths freelancers use:

ACA Marketplace plan (Healthcare.gov or your state exchange)
You could qualify for premium tax credits based on your income.
Pick a metal tier (Bronze/Silver/Gold) based on how often you use care.
Spouse/partner’s employer plan if that’s an option.
Professional associations that offer group plans.
B. Health savings tools

HSA (Health Savings Account) – if you choose a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)

Triple tax advantage:
Contributions are pre-tax (or tax-deductible).
Growth is tax-free.
Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free.
You can use it now or treat it as a stealth retirement account for future medical costs.

FSA (Flexible Spending Account)

Usually only available through an employer, so most freelancers only access this through a spouse.

System to set up:

Pick a health plan during open enrollment.
If you choose an HDHP, open an HSA at a provider that lets you invest once your balance is high enough.
Set an automatic monthly transfer into the HSA.
2. Retirement Savings (Replacing 401(k)/403(b))

Most freelancers use one or more of these:

Traditional or Roth IRA

Available to almost everyone with earned income.
Traditional: contributions may be tax-deductible; taxed later.
Roth: no deduction now; tax-free withdrawals in retirement (subject to rules).

Solo 401(k) (Individual 401(k))

For self-employed people with no employees (except a spouse).
You’re both employee and employer, so you can contribute more than to an IRA.
Can be Traditional (pre-tax) or Roth if the provider offers it.

SEP IRA

Simpler than a Solo 401(k), but only employer-style contributions (no Roth).
Good if your income is variable and you want flexibility.

System to set up:

Decide: “Do I want to be able to save a lot (Solo 401(k)/SEP) or keep it simple (IRA)?”
Open the account you choose at a brokerage.
Set a monthly auto-contribution (even a small one), then plan a year-end top-up based on your profit.
3. Disability Insurance (Income Protection)

This is the “what if I can’t work” coverage employers often give, but freelancers buy themselves.

Long-term disability insurance is the main one.
Typically replaces about 60% of your income if you can’t work due to illness or injury.
Cost depends on age, health, occupation, and how long benefits last.

System to set up:

Get quotes from multiple insurers or an independent agent.
Aim for a policy that:
Uses an own occupation definition if possible.
Has a benefit period that goes to at least age 65.
Treat the premium as a fixed line item in your monthly business budget.
4. Life Insurance

If anyone relies on your income (partner, kids, parents), freelancers usually go with:

Term life insurance (10–30 years)
Much cheaper than permanent life.
Coverage amount often based on:
Debts + future income needs + kids’ education − existing assets.

System to set up:

Decide how much coverage and how long a term would actually protect your dependents.
Get quotes for level term policies.
Put premiums on autopay from your main account.
5. Paid Time Off & Sick Leave

You don’t get PTO as a freelancer, so you pre-fund your own:

Create a “PTO savings bucket” in a high-yield savings account.
Estimate how many unpaid days you want per year (vacation, sick, holidays).
Example: 20 days off × $300/day target income = $6,000/year.
That’s $500/month into a dedicated “PTO” sub-account.

System to set up:

Open a separate savings account (or sub-account) labeled “PTO.”
Auto-transfer a fixed amount every month.
When you take time off, pay yourself from this bucket.
6. Taxes (Replacing Employer Withholding)

You’re in charge of:

Income tax
Self-employment tax (Social Security + Medicare)
Often quarterly estimated payments

System to set up:

Keep business and personal accounts separate (at least separate checking).
After each payment you receive, move a percentage into a “Tax” savings account.
Many freelancers start around 25–35% of net income, then fine-tune with a tax pro.
Pay quarterly estimated taxes (typically April, June, September, January).
Consider working with a CPA or EA who knows freelancer taxes to:
Maximize deductions (home office, equipment, health insurance, retirement contributions).
Avoid underpayment penalties.
7. Other Employer-Like Perks You Can DIY

A. Emergency fund

Aim for 3–12 months of expenses in high-yield savings, especially with variable income.

B. “Benefits-style” savings buckets

Sub-accounts for:
Equipment replacement (laptop, phone, software).
Professional development (courses, conferences).
Slow months buffer (extra cushion for income dips).

C. Legal & estate basics

Simple will, powers of attorney, and beneficiary designations on all accounts.
8. Putting It All Together: A Simple Freelancer Benefits Stack

Here’s a sample structure many freelancers use:

Big picture:
You’re recreating what an employer would normally handle using a few key accounts (HSA, IRA/Solo 401(k), insurance policies, and savings buckets) plus automation so you don’t have to think about it every week.

If you’d like this tailored, share:

Your approximate age
Rough annual freelance income
Whether you have dependents

and Monarch can turn this into a more concrete “benefits plan” with rough target amounts for each bucket
